summary:
The scramble for concessions in China during 1895–99 was an intensified phase of Western and Japanese encroachment on Chinese sovereignty following the [[event:2550|First Sino-Japanese War]], during which the major powers extracted territorial leases, railway construction rights, mining privileges, and exclusive 'spheres of influence' from a weakened Qing government. The process accelerated the crisis of the Qing state and intensified Chinese popular resentment that exploded in the [[event:2553|Boxer Rebellion]] of 1899–1901.

Japan's decisive defeat of China in 1895 demonstrated the Qing military's fundamental weakness and signaled to the Western powers that more aggressive demands would meet little effective resistance. Germany seized the bay of Jiaozhou in Shandong province in 1897 and extracted a 99-year lease and exclusive railway and mining rights in the surrounding region. Russia leased the Liaodong Peninsula and Port Arthur. France gained a sphere of influence in southern China adjacent to its Indochinese colonies. Britain leased Weihaiwei and extended its Hong Kong territory through the New Territories. Even Italy unsuccessfully attempted to obtain a concession at Sanmen Bay.

The United States, concerned that these exclusive spheres of influence would shut American commerce out of China, promoted the 'Open Door Policy' through diplomatic notes in 1899 and 1900, requesting that all powers guarantee equal commercial access to China regardless of which sphere controlled a given territory. The policy was accepted in principle by the major powers, though its practical effect was limited. The scramble for concessions represented the most acute phase of China's 'century of humiliation' and directly fed the xenophobic and anti-foreign sentiment that the Boxer movement mobilized.

read_more_links.title: Spheres of Influence in China – Britannica
